claims.claim: For Sample A (211 Ah, 342.06 Wh/kg), the critical tab temperatures for thermal runaway onset were 119°C at the positive tab and 123°C at the negative tab, defined as a temperature change rate of 1°C/s.
claims.quote: The critical temperature for TR is defined as a temperature change rate of 1 °C/s. The temperature of the positive tab rose to 119 °C, and the negative tab rose to 123 °C.

claims.claim: Sample A (211 Ah, higher energy density 342.06 Wh/kg) reached a higher peak chamber pressure of 386.5 kPa and higher peak chamber temperature of 557.5°C compared to Sample B (256 Ah, 325.82 Wh/kg) which reached 330.5 kPa and 409.86°C.
claims.quote: The highest temperature inside the chamber reached 557.5 °C, and the highest pressure inside the chamber reached 386.5 kPa. The highest temperature inside the chamber reached 409.86 °C, and the highest pressure inside the chamber was 330.5 kPa.

claims.claim: The higher-energy-density Sample A (342.06 Wh/kg) exhibited a KLIB max of 99.5 kPa·m·s−1 compared to 68.2 kPa·m·s−1 for Sample B (325.82 Wh/kg), indicating a higher explosion risk for the higher energy density battery.
claims.quote: the amplitude of the pressure variation for Sample A is significantly higher than that of Sample B... The comparatively higher explosion risk in A, as opposed to B, could be linked to A's higher energy density.

claims.claim: Both LiNi0.9Co0.05Mn0.05O2 batteries (211 Ah and 256 Ah) exhibited the same total venting duration of 32 s despite different capacities and energy densities, while their ultra-fast, fast, and slow venting stage durations were 3, 2, 27 s and 3, 3, 26 s respectively.
claims.quote: Du, Df, Ds, De being 3, 2, 27, 32 s and 3, 3, 26, 32 s, respectively

claims.claim: When thermal runaway is accompanied by more severe combustion, the electrolyte components detected in the collected gas sample decrease, as the ejected electrolyte is more completely ignited.
claims.quote: When TR is accompanied by combustion, the electrolyte components in the collected gas sample will decrease. The severity of combustion in Sample A is higher than that in Sample B. The electrolyte components ejected will also be more fully ignited.

claims.claim: Gas production normalized to standard conditions (0°C, 101 kPa) was 1.958 L/Ah for the 211 Ah battery and 2.182 L/Ah for the 256 Ah battery, indicating that gas production increases with battery capacity for the same cathode chemistry.
claims.quote: the gas production is converted to standard conditions, resulting in 1.958 L/Ah for Sample A and 2.182 L/Ah for Sample B. It can be observed that gas production is related to the capacity of the battery.

claims.claim: It took only 1 second from the opening of the safety valve (first smoke ejection) to the entire experimental chamber being filled with smoke during thermal runaway of LiNi0.9Co0.05Mn0.05O2 batteries.
claims.quote: It took only 1 s from the observation of smoke ejection to the entire chamber being filled with smoke within the field of view.

claims.claim: Sample A (211 Ah, 342.06 Wh/kg) had a mass loss rate of 73% after thermal runaway, while Sample B (256 Ah, 325.82 Wh/kg) had a mass loss rate of 64.87%, with the higher-energy-density battery showing greater mass loss.
claims.quote: The mass loss rates for A and B were 73% and 64.87%

claims.claim: The mixed vent gases from Sample A (211 Ah) had a lower flammable limit (LFL) of 8.49% and a flammable range of 49.81%, while Sample B (256 Ah) had an LFL of 7.43% and a flammable range of 44.45%.
claims.quote: Sample A: UFL 58.3, LFL 8.49, Flammable Range 49.81. Sample B: UFL 51.88, LFL 7.43, Flammable Range 44.45.

claims.claim: Compared to NCM622 batteries, the high-nickel NCM9 0.5 0.5 batteries demonstrated shorter eruption times and higher KLIB max values, attributed to higher Ni content intensifying redox reactions during thermal runaway and significantly increasing the gas generation rate.
claims.quote: Compared to Zhang's analysis of NCM6 2 2, these two batteries demonstrate shorter eruption times. This could be attributed to the higher Ni content in the samples examined in this research, intensifying the redox reactions during TR and significantly increasing the gas generation rate. Consequently, the eruption duration decreases.

claims.claim: The linearized gas flow rate during thermal runaway for the high-power 18650 cell (SonyVTC5A, NCA, 2.5 Ah) is 22.15 ± 14.70 gs−1, and for the high-energy cell (LGMJ1, NMC811, 3.5 Ah) is 27.92 ± 9.500 gs−1, both tested at 100% SoC.
claims.quote: our results show, that the linearized gas flow rate for the high-power and high-energy cell is 22.15 gs−1 and 27.92 gs−1, respectively

claims.claim: The maximum gas velocity during thermal runaway of the high-energy 18650 cell (LGMJ1, NMC811) follows a single peak asymmetrical pattern with a peak of 398.5 ± 207.1 ms−1, while the high-power cell (SonyVTC5A, NCA) shows a bumpy pattern with a maximum gas velocity of 260.9 ± 103.5 ms−1.
claims.quote: the progress of the gas velocity differs between the two cell types and in case of the high-energy cell, it follows a single peak asymmetrical pattern with a peak of 398.5 ms−1, while the high-power cell shows a bumpy pattern with a maximum gas velocity of 260.9 ms−1

claims.claim: The transition time from opening of the safety valve to thermal runaway for the high-power SonyVTC5A cells (NCA, 18650) was 69.46 ± 12.67 s, whereas the non-reheated high-energy LGMJ1 cells (NMC811, 18650) needed 189.54 ± 87.56 s, meaning the time to TR for the high-energy LIB is at least twice as long as for the high-power LIB.
claims.quote: It took 69.46 ± 12.67 s for the SonyVTC5A cells to go from opening of the safety valve into TR, whereas the non-reheated LGMJ1 cells needed 189.54 ± 87.56 s

claims.claim: The mass loss during the thermal runaway stage was 57.50% (Osci) and 56.61% (DMM) for SonyVTC5A cells, and 63.45% (Osci) and 62.89% (DMM) for LGMJ1 cells, both tested at 100% SoC with overheating as the trigger.
claims.quote: The boxplots (e) and (f) underneath show averaged mass losses with the Osci for SonyVTC5A and LGMJ1 of 57.50% and 63.45%, respectively. The values determined by the DMM are similar with 56.61% and 62.89% for SonyVTC5A and LGMJ1, respectively.

claims.claim: When the safety valve opened during overheating tests, the cell holder temperature was on average 182.01 °C for the high-power SonyVTC5A battery and 171.57 °C for the high-energy LGMJ1 battery, and the gas temperature exceeded the measurement limit of 1000 °C during the thermal runaway stage.
claims.quote: when the safety valve opens, the cell holder temperature was in average 182.01 °C for the high-power battery and 171.57 °C for the high-energy battery... the gas temperature exceeded the upper measuring limit of the data logger of 1000 °C

claims.claim: The expansion force of a 58 Ah Li(Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1)O2 prismatic battery during thermal runaway reached a peak value of 1980 N at -13.5 s, representing a 12-fold increase within a duration of 2.5 s starting from -15 s.
claims.quote: At the −15 s mark: Expansion force initiates a sharp ascent, reaching a peak value of 1980 N at −13.5 s, representing a 12-fold increase within a duration of 2.5 s.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of a 58 Ah Li(Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1)O2 prismatic battery, the maximum ejection velocity upon opening of the safety valve was 90 m/s at the 1.5-s mark, and solid particles were jetted to heights of up to 3.4 m due to incomplete combustion.
claims.quote: In the first mode, due to incomplete combustion, the generated solid particles can jet to heights of up to 3.4 m. The most intense occurrence of the first mode transpires at the 1.5-s mark, with a momentary eruption velocity of 90 m/s upon the opening of the safety valve.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of a 58 Ah Li(Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1)O2 prismatic battery, the maximum flame propagation velocity during the combustible gas explosion mode was 17.24 m/s, occurring at the 1.4-s mark.
claims.quote: The most intense event in the second mode transpires at the 1.4-s mark, with a maximum flame propagation velocity of 17.24 m/s.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of a 58 Ah Li(Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1)O2 prismatic battery, the temperature measured 1 cm above the safety release valve (TE) reached a maximum value of 941.2 °C at 7 s after voltage reached zero, while the battery surface temperature (TS) reached a maximum of 810 °C at 100 s.
claims.quote: At the 7 s mark: TE attains its maximum value of 941.2 °C, as depicted in Figure 3b; … At the 100 s mark: TS reaches its maximum value of 810 °C.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway, explosive phenomena are initiated by the ignition of ejected sparks, which ignite the combustible gases expelled during the jetting process, and the presence of solid particulates cannot be disregarded when calculating the flammability limits of battery ejecta.
claims.quote: Observations from video footage indicate that explosive phenomena are initiated by the ignition of the ejected sparks, which ignite the combustible gases expelled during the jetting process… when calculating the flammability limits of battery ejecta, the presence of solid particulates cannot be disregarded.

claims.claim: When ambient pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 20 kPa, peak HRR decreases from 1.2 kW to 0.65 kW for a single cylindrical 18650 cell, from 14.2 kW to 1.2 kW for 5 cylindrical cells, from 26.3 kW to 6.1 kW for a 10 Ah pouch cell, and from 41.1 kW to 8.7 kW for a 15 Ah pouch cell.
claims.quote: The peak HRR decreases from 1.2 kW to 0.65 kW, 14.2 kW to 1.2 kW, 26.3 kW to 6.1 kW, and 41.1 kW to 8.7 kW for 1 cylindrical cell, 5 cylindrical cells, a 10 Ah pouch cell, and a 15 Ah pouch cell, respectively.

claims.claim: When pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 20 kPa, the average TR trigger time is shortened for cylindrical cells (from 145 s to 133 s) but delayed for pouch cells (from 625 s to 643 s for 10 Ah, and from 730 s to 761 s for 15 Ah).
claims.quote: The average trigger time (t2) of spark ejections for cylindrical cells is 145 s in 95 kPa and 133 s in 20 kPa. However, t2 increases from 625 s to 643 s, and from 730 s to 761 s for 10 Ah and 15 Ah pouch cells, when the pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 20 kPa.

claims.claim: When pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 20 kPa, average peak CO values increase from 0.19 to 0.28 vol.%, 0.21 to 0.91 vol.%, 0.16 to 0.59 vol.%, and 0.24 to 0.72 vol.% for single cylindrical cell, 5 cylindrical cells, 10 Ah pouch cell, and 15 Ah pouch cell, respectively.
claims.quote: the average peak values of CO increase from 0.19 vol.% to 0.28 vol.%, 0.21 vol.% to 0.91 vol.%, 0.16 vol.% to 0.59 vol.%, and 0.24 vol.% to 0.72 vol.%, with the pressure drop from 95 kPa to 20 kPa.

claims.claim: At 20 kPa, no combustion flames occur during the entire thermal runaway process of a single cylindrical 18650 cell, because the oxygen mass concentration per volume is less than the stoichiometric ratio needed for ignition.
claims.quote: at a pressure of 20 kPa, there are no combustion flames in the whole TR process. This is probably because large amounts of combustible gases and ejections, released rapidly after the sparks (safety valve opened completely), are not ignited under 20 kPa for the reason that oxygen mass concentration per volume is less than the stoichiometric ratio.

claims.claim: Pouch cells have higher thermal hazards of TR fire (higher peak HRR and THR) but lower toxic/combustible gas emissions (lower THC and CO per unit capacity) than cylindrical cells, both at 95 kPa and 20 kPa.
claims.quote: the values of peak HRR, THR, and peak CO per unit capacity for the pouch cell are higher than the values of the cylindrical cell, but the peak values of CO/CO2, THC and CO per capacity are lower than in cylindrical cells. It indicates that the pouch cell with a soft aluminum–polymer bag has higher thermal hazards of TR fire, but a lower concentration of toxic/combustible gases than cylindrical cells with hard cans.

claims.claim: When pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 20 kPa, the average first venting time (safety valve opening or bag rupture) decreases from 144 s to 133 s for 18650 cylindrical cells, from 475 s to 461 s for 10 Ah pouch cells, and from 559 s to 533 s for 15 Ah pouch cells.
claims.quote: the average values of t1 (the average open time of the safety valve or the rupture time of the bag) decrease from 144 s to 133 s, 475 s to 461 s, and 559 s to 533 s, for 18650 cylindrical cells, 10 Ah pouch cells, and 15 Ah pouch cells, respectively.

claims.claim: Under 20 kPa, average peak THC values are higher than under 95 kPa for all test groups: 1275 vs. 1019 ppm for single cylindrical cell, 3474 vs. 256 ppm for 5 cylindrical cells, 819 vs. 127 ppm for 10 Ah pouch cell, and 1641 vs. 559 ppm for 15 Ah pouch cell.
claims.quote: The average peak THC values in 20 kPa are about 1275 ppm, 3474 ppm, 819 ppm, and 1641 ppm for four groups of tests, which can be seen in Figure 13b, which are higher than 1019 ppm, 256 ppm, 127 ppm, and 559 ppm under 95 kPa, respectively.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway intensity of individual cells decreases under lower ambient pressure, but the burning intensity increases dramatically when thermal runaway occurs in a battery pack.
claims.quote: The thermal runaway intensity of individual cells decreases under lower ambient pressure, but the burning intensity increases dramatically when thermal runaway occurs in a battery pack.

claims.claim: LFP tests did not flame at 0% and 30% SOCs, while LCO tests flamed at all SOCs, consistent with LFP cells having higher thermal stability compared to LCO cells.
claims.quote: LFP tests did not flame at 0% and 30% SOCs, while LCO tests flamed at all SOCs (Figure 5b). This is consistent with LFP cells having higher thermal stability compared to LCO cells.

claims.claim: While OC was more abundant during cell venting, EC and PO4 3− were more abundant when flaming combustion occurred.
claims.quote: While OC was more abundant during cell venting, EC and PO4 3− were more abundant when flaming combustion occurred.

claims.claim: For LFP tests at 50% and 75% SOCs dominated by flaming combustion, PO4 3− dominated with 34–44% of PM2.5 mass, while OM fractions were much lower at 1–20% compared to 0% and 30% SOC cases.
claims.quote: Compared to the 0% and 30% SOC cases, the OM fractions were much lower (1–20%), while the PO4 3− fraction, practically absent in the 0% and 30% SOC cases, dominated with 34–44% of PM2.5 mass.

claims.claim: Ignition of the emissions stream, rather than cell SOC, is mostly responsible for the general composition of emitted PM2.5 particles.
claims.quote: This indicates that ignition of the emissions stream, rather than cell SOC, is mostly responsible for the general composition of emitted PM2.5 particles.

claims.claim: For LCO tests, LCO anion/cation ratios were directly dependent on SOC: 80–100% SOCs had ratios just above 1:1, while 0–60% SOCs had ratios near 20:1, indicating the freshly emitted particles were acidic.
claims.quote: LCO anion/cation ratios were directly dependent on SOC. With one exception (Test P7), 80–100% SOCs had ratios just above 1:1, while 0–60% SOCs had ratios near 20:1.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway is characterized by rapid self-heating starting at around 150–220 °C and increasing to about 1000 °C.
claims.quote: thermal runaway is characterised by rapid self-heating starting at around 150–220 ◦C and increasing to about 1000 ◦C

claims.claim: The first step towards thermal runaway is commonly the breakdown of the SEI layer on the graphite anode, which occurs at about 90–120 °C.
claims.quote: the first step towards TR is commonly the breakdown of the SEI layer on the graphite anode, which occurs at about 90–120 ◦C

claims.claim: When the separator melts at temperatures in the range of 135–165 °C, the electrical contact between the anode and cathode can result in an internal short circuit and self-discharge.
claims.quote: When the separator melts at temperatures in the range of 135–165 ◦C, the electrical contact between the anode and cathode can result in an ISC and, thereby, self-discharge.

claims.claim: As temperature reaches 200 °C and higher, all components of the electrolyte start to decompose exothermically, forming CO2, HF, hydrocarbons, and various fluorinated hydrocarbon gases.
claims.quote: As the temperature reaches 200 ◦C and higher, all components of the electrolyte start to decompose exothermically, upon which CO2, hydrogen fluoride (HF), hydrocarbons, and various fluorinated hydrocarbon gases are formed.

claims.claim: The cathode begins to decompose and release oxygen as temperature increases in the range of 150–310 °C.
claims.quote: As the temperature increases further in the range of 150–310 ◦C, the cathode begins to decompose and release oxygen.

claims.claim: The total vented gas volume from LIB cells scales linearly with cell capacity.
claims.quote: A clear trend found was that the gas volume scaled linearly with cell capacity

claims.claim: Reactive gases O2 and H2 are dissolved in the electrolyte to a lesser extent than the more stable CO2 and hydrocarbons, making them more available to react during a thermal event.
claims.quote: the reactive gases O2 and H2 are dissolved in the electrolyte to a lesser extent than the more stable CO2 and hydrocarbons. This means that the reactive gases are, to a larger extent, available to react with additional gases formed during a thermal event

claims.claim: Aged LIB cells show a different thermal response and different gas composition compared to fresh cells when thermal events are triggered.
claims.quote: Studies where thermal events were triggered in aged LIBs show that aged cells can have a different thermal response and different gas composition compared to fresh cells

claims.claim: For NCA cells at low SOC, CO2 dominated the vent gas composition, while CO increased and dominated at SOC greater than 60%, but the sum of CO and CO2 remained consistent at about 70% for all cells.
claims.quote: An important trend was that at a low SOC, CO2 dominated, while CO increased and dominated at a SOC greater than 60%. However, the sum of the CO and CO2 was consistent at about 70% for all cells.

claims.claim: Slow thermal runaway initiation such as homogeneous heating of the entire cell favours higher relative amounts of CO2, whereas fast triggers like nail penetration or high-power localised heaters typically lead to incomplete oxidation and higher relative concentrations of CO.
claims.quote: Slow TR initiation, such as the homogeneous heating of the entire cell, favours higher relative amounts of CO2, whereas fast triggers, like nail penetration or high-power localised heaters, typically lead to incomplete oxidation and higher relative concentrations of CO.

claims.claim: Single-cell results cannot be extrapolated linearly to multi-cell configurations due to the higher temperatures realised in the latter case, which directly impacts chemical processing and reaction rates.
claims.quote: single-cell results cannot be extrapolated linearly to multi-cell configurations due to the higher temperatures realised in the latter case, which directly impacts the chemical processing and rates of reaction.

claims.claim: For highly reactive species such as HF and POF3, the presence of additional surfaces in multi-cell assemblies and batteries reduces the actual gas concentrations due to rapid adsorption and reaction.
claims.quote: For highly reactive species, such as HF and POF3, the presence of additional surfaces in multi-cell assemblies and batteries reduces the actual gas concentrations due to rapid adsorption and reaction.

claims.claim: LFP cathodes likely produce very little or no oxygen at all, and the reactivity of oxygen-producing cathodes is ranked as LCO > NCA > NCM > LMO.
claims.quote: Lithium–iron phosphate (LFP) cathodes likely produce very little or no oxygen at all, which is represented by the half-cell (cathode) chemistry with the lowest risk going into TR. The reactivity of the oxygen-producing cathodes is ranked as follows: LCO > NCA > NCM > LMO

claims.claim: For NCA cells heated to 150 °C versus 160 °C, the additional 10 °C led to more than a doubling of the gas volume, indicating significant internal processing occurred at this temperature.
claims.quote: there was quite a large difference between cells quenched at 150 ◦C and 160 ◦C; the additional 10 ◦C led to more than a doubling of the gas volume, indicating that significant internal processing occurred in the cell at this temperature.

claims.claim: In LFP cells, CO concentrations are low, below 10% for most cases, and there is no significant increase in CO with increasing SOC, while CO2 concentrations show a decreasing trend with increasing SOC.
claims.quote: A clear trend seen in Figure 2 is that CO concentrations are low, below 10% for most cases, including both inert and air environments. There is no significant increase in CO with increasing SOC. CO2 concentrations, as well as H2 concentrations (Figure 3), are greatly scattered between the datasets, but within each dataset, the trend is decreasing CO2 with increasing SOC.

claims.claim: Rapid nail penetration triggering leads to thermal runaway on very short timescales with no preceding first vent.
claims.quote: Rapid penetration triggering leads to TR on very short timescales, with no preceding first vent

claims.claim: When overcharge was used to trigger thermal runaway, the first venting occurred at or below 80 °C as measured at the cell surface.
claims.quote: When overcharge was used to trigger TR, the venting occurred at or below 80 ◦C (measured at the surface of the cell).

claims.claim: For temperature triggers, the first vent in LIB cells occurred at temperatures from 120 to 195 °C, with NMC and NCA cells in the lower part of this range.
claims.quote: For temperature triggers, the first vent occurred at temperatures from 120 to 195 ◦C, with the NMC and NCA cells in the lower part of this range.

claims.claim: The gas volume of the first vent was not dependent on SOC, based on a study with SOC varied at lower values.
claims.quote: the only study with a lower SOC indicated that the gas volume of the first vent was not dependent on SOC

claims.claim: LIB fires cannot be sustained by internally released oxygen present in the vent gas mixture; open flame fire relies on the availability of atmospheric oxygen.
claims.quote: it can be concluded that it is very unlikely that LIB fires can be sustained by the internally released oxygen present in the gas mixture and that open flame fire relies on the availability of atmospheric oxygen.

claims.claim: EMC shows signs of thermal decomposition already at 175 °C in the presence of 1.2 M LiPF6, while DMC is not affected by salt presence up to about 450 °C.
claims.quote: With 1.2 M of LiPF6, EMC shows signs of thermal decomposition already at 175 ◦C, while DMC is not affected.

claims.claim: For LFP cells, O2 production from the cathode material was not expected in the first vents, which was confirmed in studies instrumented to detect O2.
claims.quote: The internal production of O2 gas from the cathode material was initiated at higher temperatures and was not expected in the first vents, which was confirmed in the studies that were instrumented to detect O2

claims.claim: Over a full range of SOCs for LFP, NMC, and NCA LIBs, the relative concentration of inert CO2 decreases and more reactive components including CO increase as SOC increases.
claims.quote: over the full range of SOCs, the relative concentration of inert CO2 decreases and the more reactive components, including CO, increase as SOC increases.

claims.claim: The only CO/CO2 ratio that can be considered representative of vent gas composition is one measured in an inert atmosphere, as oxidation of CO to CO2 likely occurs in the presence of O2 in non-inert environments.
claims.quote: only the CO/CO2 ratio measured in an inert atmosphere can be considered representative of the vent gas composition.

claims.claim: Approximately 6% of produced gas volume from cathode material of an overcharged NCA cell can be O2, and based on reaction kinetics, this amount is completely consumed by chemical reactions inside the LIB cell.
claims.quote: Golubkov et al. [39] used a simplified calculation to establish that about 6% of the produced gas volume from the cathode material of an over-charged NCA cell can be O2. Based on reaction kinetics, an amount this small will be completely consumed by chemical reactions inside the LIB cell.

claims.claim: The critical surface temperatures at thermal runaway onset (Tf-TR) for 50 Ah batteries under lateral heating at 100% SOC are: LFP (346°C) > NIBs (292°C) > NCM523 (290°C) > NCM622 (281°C).
claims.quote: The critical temperature for thermal runaway (Tf-TR) of the batteries near the heating plate are as follows: LiFePO4 (LFP) battery: 346 °C, Sodium-ion battery (NIBs): 292 °C, NCM523 battery: 290 °C, NCM622 battery: 281 °C.

claims.claim: The heat input required to trigger thermal runaway in 50 Ah batteries ranks as: LFP (592.8 kJ) > NCM523 (274 kJ) > NCM622 (262 kJ) > NIBs (158 kJ), with NIBs requiring the least energy and LFP the most.
claims.quote: The heat injected into the batteries is as follows: 592.8 kJ for the LFP battery, 274 kJ for the NCM523 battery, and 262 kJ for the NCM622 battery. A higher value of Q indicates that thermal runaway in the battery is more difficult to trigger. The heat input to the batteries, from largest to smallest, is: LFP > NCM622 > NCM523 > NIBs. The NIBs battery has the lowest triggering energy for thermal runaway, while the LFP battery has the highest triggering energy, making it the safest.

claims.claim: For NCM622 and NCM523 batteries at 100% SOC, the safety valve opening and the onset of violent thermal runaway occur almost simultaneously, making it nearly impossible to use safety valve opening as an early warning indicator.
claims.quote: for the two types of NCM lithium batteries, due to their higher energy density, the opening of the safety valve and the intense thermal runaway (sharp temperature rise) occur simultaneously. Therefore, it is almost impossible to use the opening of the safety valve as an early warning indicator for thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway propagation rates within 50 Ah batteries rank as: NCM523 (3.375 mm/s) > NCM622 (2.7 mm/s) > NIBs (1.726 mm/s) > LFP (1.241 mm/s), with LFP exhibiting the lowest propagation rate.
claims.quote: The propagation rate for the sodium-ion battery is 1.726 mm/s, for the LFP battery is 1.241 mm/s, for the NCM523 battery is 3.375 mm/s, and for the NCM622 battery is 2.7 mm/s. The propagation rates from largest to smallest are: NCM523 > NCM622 > NIBs > LFP.

claims.claim: Among four 50 Ah battery types, NIBs have the lowest lower flammability limit (LFL = 3.39%) of mixed thermal runaway gases, indicating the highest explosion risk, primarily due to significant propylene content in the vented gases.
claims.quote: It was found that the sodium-ion battery had the lower flammability limit, with an LFL value of 3.98. This is primarily because the gases produced during the thermal runaway of the sodium-ion battery contain a significant amount of propylene (C3H6), which has the lower flammability limit. Consequently, the mixed gases from the sodium-ion battery exhibit the lower flammability limit, resulting in the highest explosion hazard.

claims.claim: NCM433 initiates thermal decomposition at 245°C, whereas NCM811 begins at 135°C, with higher nickel content correlating with lower thermal decomposition onset temperatures and greater oxygen release.
claims.quote: higher nickel content correlates with lower thermal decomposition onset temperatures and greater oxygen release. For example, NCM433 initiates decomposition at 245 °C, whereas NCM811 begins at 135 °C

claims.claim: For all NCM materials, singlet oxygen (1O2) evolution initiates when SOC exceeds 80%, independent of electrode potential but dependent on lithium extraction levels.
claims.quote: For all NCM materials, singlet oxygen (1O2) evolution initiates when SOC exceeds 80%, independent of electrode potential but dependent on lithium extraction levels.

claims.claim: Lattice oxygen loss of 1% in NCM cathodes causes up to 21% capacity fade.
claims.quote: Lattice oxygen loss not only drives gas evolution, but also severely degrades capacity, with 1% oxygen loss causing up to 21% capacity fade

claims.claim: In LiCoO2-based cells tested at 90°C, significant gas evolution (primarily CO2, CO, and C2H4) occurs when the state of charge exceeds approximately 80% during charging.
claims.quote: Testing of LiCoO2-based cells at 90 °C revealed significant gas evolution (primarily CO2, CO, and C2H4) when the state of charge (SOC) exceeds ~80% during charging

claims.claim: Pure LiPF6 decomposes at 107°C in dry environments but remains stable at 90°C; in the presence of trace water (300 ppm), decomposition occurs at 87°C.
claims.quote: pure LiPF6 decomposes at 107 °C in dry environments, but remains stable at 90 °C. In the presence of trace water (300 ppm), decomposition occurs at 87 °C

claims.claim: The incorporation of high-specific-surface-area conductive carbon agents reduces the onset temperature of thermal runaway by approximately 20°C.
claims.quote: The incorporation of such conductive agents reduces the onset temperature of thermal runaway by approximately 20 °C, compromises the chemical stability of the system, and promotes gas generation through accelerated decomposition reactions

claims.claim: In LFP overcharge testing, significant gas release initiates at approximately 120% SOC, and at 130% SOC substantial gas evolution occurs accompanied by a temperature rise to approximately 140°C, followed by HF generation during cooling.
claims.quote: Overcharge testing of LFP cells reveals gas evolution, including CO2, H2, C2H4, CO, and C2H2F, with significant gas release initiating at ~120% SOC (Figure 4b). At 130% SOC, substantial gas evolution occurs, accompanied by a temperature rise to ~140 °C, followed by HF generation during cooling

claims.claim: The SEI decomposes exothermically at 90–120°C, with metastable components such as (CH2OCO2Li)2 decomposing to produce C2H4, CO2, and O2.
claims.quote: The SEI decomposes exothermically at 90–120 °C, with metastable components such as (CH2OCO2Li)2 decomposing to produce C2H4, CO2, and O2 via the reaction: 2(CH2OCO2Li)2 → 2Li2CO3 + 2C2H4 + 2CO2 + O2

claims.claim: Adding vinylene carbonate (VC) as an electrolyte additive reduces H2 evolution to 13% of its original level and reduces ethylene release by 60%, but generates CO2 as a byproduct.
claims.quote: adding vinylene carbonate (VC) forms a protective SEI layer, reducing H2 evolution to 13% of its original level. VC additives also reduce ethylene release by 60% but generate CO2 as a byproduct

claims.claim: Silicon-based anodes exhibit accelerated gas generation during early thermal runaway due to exothermic Si-Li alloy reactions with ΔH > 1500 J/g, where released H2 reacts with electrolyte decomposition products (e.g., PF5) to form toxic gases (e.g., HPF6).
claims.quote: silicon-based anodes exhibit accelerated gas generation during early thermal runaway due to exothermic Si-Li alloy reactions (∆H > 1500 J/g), where released H2 reacts with electrolyte decomposition products (e.g., PF5) to form toxic gases (e.g., HPF6)

claims.claim: During thermal runaway, elevated temperatures above 80°C induce SEI decomposition, releasing heat and flammable gases (e.g., H2), which subsequently form explosive mixtures with cathode-derived oxygen.
claims.quote: elevated temperatures (>80 °C) induce SEI decomposition, releasing heat and flammable gases (e.g., H2), which subsequently form explosive mixtures with cathode-derived oxygen

claims.claim: Above 250°C, significant HF, fluoroalkanes (e.g., C2H5F), and ethers (e.g., dimethyl ether) are generated due to oxygen release from NCM cathodes, whereas below 250°C thermal failure species remain largely unchanged.
claims.quote: Below 250 °C, thermal failure species remain largely unchanged; however, above 250 °C, significant HF, fluoroalkanes (e.g., C2H5F), and ethers (e.g., dimethyl ether) are generated due to oxygen release from NCM cathodes.

claims.claim: In LFP STA-MS testing, no O2 release occurs below 600°C from a single LFP cathode, and the exothermic reaction occurring after 500°C correlates with CO2 generation likely caused by a chemical reaction between the cathode and the graphite coating on its surface.
claims.quote: the STA-MS test results for a single cathode indicate no O2 release below 600 °C. Additionally, the exothermic reaction occurring after 500 °C correlates with the generation of CO2, which is likely caused by a chemical reaction between the cathode and the graphite coating on its surface.

claims.claim: Residual Li2CO3 on NCM cathode surfaces decomposes at 3.8 V to generate highly reactive singlet oxygen (1O2), not released as molecular oxygen (O2), via the reaction: 2Li2CO3 → 4Li+ + 4e− + 2CO2 + 1O2.
claims.quote: it was discovered that Li2CO3 decomposes at 3.8 V to generate highly reactive 1O2, which is not released as molecular oxygen (O2). The decomposition reaction is described as: 2Li2CO3→4Li++ 4e−+ 2CO2+ 1O2

claims.claim: In β-Li3PS4 solid electrolyte cells with indium anodes paired with NCM cathodes cycled at 45°C, no H2S evolution was detected, but SO2 release was observed closely linked to O2 evolution from decomposition of surface carbonates reacting with β-Li3PS4.
claims.quote: During normal cycling at 45 °C, no H2S evolution was detected in cells with Li2TiO3 or indium anodes paired with NCM cathodes. However, SO2 release was observed in cells with indium anodes, closely linked to O2 evolution. The decomposition of surface carbonates generates highly reactive oxygen species, which react with β-Li3PS4 to form SO2.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of a prismatic 51 Ah NMC622 cell at 100% SOC, approximately 60% of cell mass was lost as vent gases and particles.
claims.quote: During TR, the cells lost approximately 60% of their mass as vent gases and particles.

claims.claim: Re-solidified copper droplets found in ejected particles indicate that local internal cell temperatures during thermal runaway exceeded 1085°C, the melting temperature of copper.
claims.quote: The latter require inside cell TR temperatures that, at least locally, exceed 1085 ◦C, the melting temperature of copper.

claims.claim: Based on intact separator foil pieces and molten copper droplets, particle temperatures during ejection from the cell ranged from approximately 200°C to over 1100°C.
claims.quote: Based on intact separator foil pieces and molten copper droplets, particle temperatures during their ejection from the cell varied from about 200 ◦C to over 1100 ◦C.

claims.claim: Graphite combustion is the main exothermic event in ejected abuse particles, occurring between 500–750°C and causing a mass loss of approximately 10–30% depending on particle size.
claims.quote: Simultaneous thermal analysis of the seven smallest size fractions revealed graphite combustion as the main exothermic event between 500–750 ◦C, causing a mass loss of ~10–30% depending on particle size.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway heat release for the 51 Ah NMC622 cell at 100% SOC was estimated at approximately 1 MJ, corresponding to approximately 1.5 times the electrical energy stored in the cell.
claims.quote: Based on an energy balance that considers the increase in internal energy of all setup components, the TR heat release can be estimated to be about 1 MJ, which correlates to approximately 1.5 times the electrical energy stored in the cell at 100% SOC.

claims.claim: Sphericity, symmetry, and aspect ratio of ejected thermal runaway particles decrease for larger particles, with volume-based median sphericity of 0.77, median symmetry of 0.90, and median aspect ratio of 0.71 for particles smaller than 2 mm.
claims.quote: Particles are clearly non-spherical, with sphericity, symmetry, and aspect ratio decreasing for larger particles... The volume-based median sphericity is 0.77... a volume-based median symmetry of 0.90... with a volume-based median value of 0.71.

claims.claim: A total of 168 metallic conductive particles with a maximum Feret diameter above 10 mm were identified in sample #3, with the largest particle reaching 51.4 mm.
claims.quote: A total of 168 metallic particles with a maximum Feret diameter above 10 mm could be identified in sample #3, with the largest particle reaching 51.4 mm.

claims.claim: The exothermic reaction onset temperature for ejected abuse particles in oxidative atmosphere is approximately 500–600°C, with peak heat release between 600–750°C.
claims.quote: The reaction onset of the latter was determined to be around 500–600 ◦C in this study, with peak heat release between 600–750 ◦C.

claims.claim: For ejected abuse particles smaller than 250 µm, a minor endothermic mass loss of maximum 1% was observed up to 250°C, attributed to evaporation of volatile species such as residual electrolyte, with smaller particles showing higher mass loss.
claims.quote: Up to 250 ◦C a slightly increasing DSC and a slightly decreasing TG signal can be observed. The minor endothermic mass loss of maximum 1% can be related to the evaporation of volatile species such as residual electrolyte, with smaller particles having a higher mass loss.

claims.claim: Ninety-five percent of ejected abuse particles from the prismatic NMC622 cell have a size below 1 mm, while 60% are smaller than 63 µm based on mass-weighted sieve analysis.
claims.quote: Ninety-five percent of the particles have a size below 1 mm, while 60% of the particles are smaller than 63 µm.

claims.claim: Of the three cell chemistries tested under OT-2 side trigger, LFP releases the least normalized vent gas at 0.02 mol/Ah, followed by NVPF at 0.05 mol/Ah, and NMC at the highest at 0.07 mol/Ah.
claims.quote: Of the three cell chemistries, LFP releases the least amount of vent gas at 0.02 mol/Ah (41% H2, 27% CO2, 8% CO), followed by NVPF at 0.05 mol/Ah (42% CO2, 17% electrolyte solvent, 15% H2 and 10% CO) and NMC at 0.07 mol/Ah (36% CO, 24% CO2, 19% H2).

claims.claim: The maximum vent gas temperature increases from NVPF (265°C) to LFP (446°C) to NMC (1050°C) under OT-2 side trigger conditions.
claims.quote: The maximum vent gas temperature increases from NVPF (265 ◦C) to LFP (446 ◦C) and NMC (1050 ◦C).

claims.claim: Among NVPF trigger methods, overcharge produces the highest vent gas at 1.23 mol (0.07 mol/Ah) and the highest H2 concentration at 23%, while external short circuit produces the lowest vent gas at 0.14 mol.
claims.quote: The overcharge trigger produces the most amount of vent gas, 1.23 mol... this trigger exhibits the highest recorded H2 (23%) concentration... The lowest amount of vent gas is measured for the externally shorted-circuited cell, at 0.14 mol.

claims.claim: The flammability window of NVPF vent gas mixture (7%–28%) is narrower than that of NMC (7%–46%) and LFP (6%–45%), indicating reduced explosion risk for NVPF vent gas.
claims.quote: This window is between 7% and 28% for the vent gas mixture of the NVPF cell. The Li-ion cells have a calculated flammability window of 7% to 46% and 6% to 45% for the NMC and LFP chemistry, respectively.

claims.claim: Milder trigger conditions from nail penetration and external short circuit result in lower vent gas production because not all decomposition reactions take place due to lower or no additional energy input into the cell.
claims.quote: The milder TR conditions of the nail penetration and external short trigger, in comparison to the other triggers, results from lower or no additional energy input into the cell. As a consequence, not all decomposition reactions take place, resulting in lower vent gas production.

claims.claim: Increasing ventilation rate decreases gas concentration and shortens the duration of battery pack exposure to explosion risk during thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: increasing ventilation rate can decrease the gas concentration and shorten the duration of battery pack under explosion

claims.claim: Increasing cell distance reduces thermal runaway propagation speed but inhibits rapid dispersion of venting gases, causing the battery pack to be exposed to a prolonged explosion risk.
claims.quote: Despite of reducing TR propagation speeds, the increase of cell distance can inhibit the rapid dispersion of venting gases, causing battery pack exposed to a prolonged explosion risk.

claims.claim: Void volume has a limited effect on the duration of battery pack exposure to explosion risk during thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: a limited effect of void volume is evident for that

claims.claim: For a battery pack without active ventilation, ejected gases achieve a saturation condition and gas concentrations remain constant as thermal runaway propagates, with maximum concentrations reaching 0.177, 0.157, 0.135, and 0.064 g/L for H2, CO, CO2, and CH4 respectively.
claims.quote: the ejected gases could ultimately achieve a saturation condition that they flowed straight out after being ejected, rather than accumulating in the convergence plenum. Therefore, the gas concentrations were basically constant with the increasing number of TR cells, the maximum of which could attain 0.177 g⋅L−1, 0.157 g⋅L−1, 0.135 g⋅L−1 and 0.064 g⋅L−1

claims.claim: The anti-explosion performance order of flow channel layouts in air-cooling battery packs is Z-type > U-type > I-type, with dimensionless time indices of 0.529, 0.98, and 0.99 respectively.
claims.quote: The time index τ were calculated as 0.529, 0.98 and 0.99 for the Z-type, U-type and I-type ACBP, respectively, which is the evidence of a better safety performance for Z-type compared with other designs.

claims.claim: Increasing cell-to-cell distance from 1 mm to 2 mm reduces thermal runaway propagation speed from 0.128 mm/s to 0.084 mm/s, with average propagation times of 218 s, 292 s, and 359 s for 1 mm, 1.5 mm, and 2 mm gaps respectively.
claims.quote: The average time of TR propagating from a failure cell to its adjected cell was about 218 s, 292 s and 359 s for the battery module with 1 mm, 1.5 mm and 2 mm channel gaps. Correspondingly, the propagation speeds were calculated as 0.128 mm⋅s−1, 0.099 mm⋅s−1 and 0.084 mm⋅s−1.

claims.claim: When the ventilation rate was 150 L/h, the duration of the battery pack under explosion risk was approximately 28 s, indicating this ventilation rate is capable of eliminating explosion or fire hazard after thermal runaway.
claims.quote: When the ventilation rate was high as 150 L⋅h−1, the duration of the battery pack under explosion risk is just approximately 28 s, which indicated such a ventilation rate is capable of the elimination of explosion or fire hazard after TR.

claims.claim: Without ventilation, the maximum combustible gas concentration exceeded 64.8% while the minimum LEL was only 7.7% during thermal runaway propagation, and the battery pack was not relieved from explosion hazard until 1365 s.
claims.quote: When the battery pack was not ventilated, the maximal combustible gas concentrations is over than 64.8 % while the minimal LEL is only 7.7 % during TR propagation. The final venting event ended at 1132 s and then the concentrations of combustible gases gradually dropped... The battery pack was not relieved from the explosion hazard until the concentrations of the combustible gases decreased below LEL at 1365 s.

claims.claim: The safety valve is assumed to open when cell temperature rises to 167.2°C, based on experimental data.
claims.quote: it is assumed that the safety valve opens when the cell temperature rises to 167.2 ◦C according to Zhang's experiments [11]

claims.claim: The coupled thermal resistance network and CFD model reduced calculation time by approximately 68.9% compared with the traditional fluid-solid coupling CFD model, with mesh numbers reduced from 384,000 to 135,000.
claims.quote: the grid points of new model are reduced from 384,000 to 135,000 compared with traditional CFD model based on fluid-solid coupling, which also leads to the boost of calculation efficiency that the calculation time is shortened by approximately 68.9 %.

claims.claim: NMC-based cells emit higher volumes of hazardous gases and propagate thermal runaway more readily than LFP cells, indicating chemistry-dependent risk profiles.
claims.quote: comparative experiments reveal that NMC-based cells emit higher volumes of hazardous gases and propagate TR more readily than LFP cells, indicating chemistry-dependent risk profiles

claims.claim: Using conductive nails in nail penetration tests causes greater temperature spikes than non-conductive nails.
claims.quote: Abaza et al. found that using conductive nails in NP tests causes greater temperature spikes than non-conductive ones

claims.claim: A study comparing various thermal runaway initiation methods found different quantities and concentrations of emitted gases even for the same cell chemistry.
claims.quote: A study comparing various TR initiation methods found different quantities and concentrations of emitted gases even for the same cell chemistry

claims.claim: Hard-case prismatic cells can withstand a higher internal pressure of 855 kPa before venting, compared to pouch cells which vent at 411 kPa, under inert atmosphere nail penetration conditions.
claims.quote: hard-case prismatic cells could withstand a higher internal pressure (i.e., 855 kPa) before venting, compared to pouch cells (411 kPa). As the tests were conducted in an inert atmosphere, no fires were reported

claims.claim: For NMC pouch cells at 50% SOC, only visible swelling was observed during nail penetration tests, while cells at higher SOCs had thermal runaway triggered by nail penetration.
claims.quote: SOC played a significant role in the NP results: for cells at 50% SOC, only visible swelling was observed, while cells at higher SOCs had TR triggered by NP

claims.claim: Vent gases ignited under an oxygen-rich atmosphere during nail penetration tests, while under inert nitrogen the ejecta glowed but did not ignite.
claims.quote: Diekmann et al. reported that vent gases ignited under an oxygen-rich atmosphere, while under inert nitrogen, the ejecta glowed but did not ignite

claims.claim: Cells that ignited during nail penetration tests released greater amounts of CO2 compared to cells that did not ignite.
claims.quote: The cause of this disparity is unclear, but it had a direct effect on gas emissions, particularly CO2 and CO concentrations. Cells that ignited released greater amounts of CO2

claims.claim: LFP cells of different capacities were found not to undergo failure during nail penetration tests, while NMC cells reached maximum temperatures ranging from 1010 °C to 1038 °C under NP-induced thermal runaway.
claims.quote: LFP cells of different capacities were found not to undergo failure during NP tests, let alone a TR... they found that the NMC cells reached a maximum temperature ranging from 1010 °C to 1038 °C under NP-induced TR

claims.claim: A penetration speed of 1 mm/s, much lower than the 80 mm/s suggested in SAE standards, could significantly lower standard deviations in temperature and voltage profiles during nail penetration tests.
claims.quote: Diekmann et al. examined the influence of penetration speed (e.g., 1 mm/s, 5 mm/s, 10 mm/s, 40 mm/s, and 80 mm/s) and found that using a penetration speed of 1 mm/s, much lower than the 80 mm/s suggested in SAE standards, could significantly lower standard deviations in temperature and voltage profiles

claims.claim: Faster nail penetration rates may limit residence time required for intermediate reactions, leading to a higher fraction of unreacted solvent vapors in the final emission mixture from nail penetration tests.
claims.quote: Faster penetration rates (e.g., 80 mm/s) may limit the residence time required for intermediate reactions, leading to a higher fraction of unreacted solvent vapors in the final emission mixture

claims.claim: Nail geometry (diameter 2–5 mm and tip angles 20–40°) did not significantly affect whether thermal runaway occurred or how severe it was during nail penetration at a low penetration speed of 1 mm/s.
claims.quote: based on a NP study on LCO cells using various nail diameters (i.e., 2–5 mm) and tip angles (i.e., 20–40°), it was found that nail geometry did not significantly affect whether TR occurred or how severe it was. The authors attributed the outcomes to the dominant influence of the low penetration speed (i.e., 1 mm/s)

claims.claim: In nitrogen-filled chamber nail penetration tests, the battery reached a maximum temperature of 47 °C, compared to 122 °C in an air-filled chamber, an 88.7% increase.
claims.quote: Diekmann et al. reported that in a nitrogen-filled chamber, the battery only reached a maximum temperature of 47 °C, compared to 122 °C in an air-filled chamber, an 88.7% increase

claims.claim: In nail penetration tests, air environments resulted in higher CO2 and lower H2 concentrations, while N2 environments produced the opposite trend.
claims.quote: the concentrations of H2 and CO2 can differ in tests conducted in air or inert environments: air environments resulted in higher CO2 and lower H2 concentrations, while N2 environments produced the opposite trend

claims.claim: Increased humidity within a N2 environment during nail penetration tests promoted condensation of vapour-phase products on chamber surfaces, reducing the concentration of measurable gaseous species.
claims.quote: Their results showed that increased humidity within a N2 environment promoted condensation of vapour-phase products on chamber surfaces, reducing the concentration of measurable gaseous species

claims.claim: Total gas generation per unit capacity from NMC prismatic cells in nail penetration tests ranged from 1.77 to 2.06 L/Ah across studies testing cells at 60 Ah, 8–145 Ah, and 157 Ah.
claims.quote: the total gas generation per unit capacity ranged from 1.77 to 2.06 L/Ah, suggesting a correlation between cell capacity and gas evolution

claims.claim: NMC modules reached up to 900 °C during nail penetration tests, while LFP modules peaked at only 175 °C.
claims.quote: NMC modules were found to reach up to 900 °C, while LFP modules peaked at only 175 ◦C

claims.claim: For NMC prismatic cells at 100% SOC, those tested in an open setup reached peak temperatures between 665 °C and 725 °C, while those tested inside a closed N2-chamber exhibited much higher values ranging from 838 °C to 857 °C.
claims.quote: For NMC prismatic cells at 100% SOC, the ones tested in an open setup reached peak temperatures between 665 °C and 725 °C, while those tested inside a closed N2-chamber exhibited much higher values, ranging from 838 °C to 857 °C

claims.claim: The separator melting temperature for polyethylene (PE) is approximately 135 °C, whereas polypropylene (PP) exhibits a melting point around 165 °C.
claims.quote: The melting point of PE is approximately 135 °C, whereas PP exhibits higher thermal resistance, with a melting point around 165 °C

claims.claim: PP separators can shrink by approximately 50% at around 170 °C, but this thermal shrinkage can be significantly mitigated by applying a ceramic coating.
claims.quote: Experimental studies have shown that PP separators can shrink by approximately 50% at around 170 °C, though this thermal shrinkage can be significantly mitigated by applying a ceramic coating

claims.claim: LFP cathodes at 100% SOC exhibit an onset of heat release between 180 °C and 250 °C, with peak heating occurring between 210 °C and 360 °C.
claims.quote: LFP cathodes at 100% SOC exhibit an onset of heat release between 180 °C and 250 °C, with peak heating occurring between 210 °C and 360 °C

claims.claim: Total heat generation increases non-linearly while the onset temperature of thermal runaway decreases with increasing state-of-charge in NCA-based Li-ion cells.
claims.quote: total heat generation increases non-linearly, while the onset temperature of TR decreases with SOC

claims.claim: Carbon monoxide concentration during thermal runaway is directly correlated to the state-of-charge of the battery.
claims.quote: carbon monoxide has been identified as one of the primary constituents, with its concentration directly correlated to the SOC of the battery

claims.claim: LFP batteries emit significantly higher amounts of CO during thermal runaway—up to an order of magnitude greater—than LCO and NMC batteries.
claims.quote: lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries emit significantly higher amounts–up to an order of magnitude greater than LCO and NMC batteries

claims.claim: If sufficient air is present and the SOC exceeds 50%, the toxic gas vapor from thermal runaway typically ignites within one minute.
claims.quote: If sufficient air is present and the SOC exceeds 50%, this toxic gas vapor typically ignites within one minute

claims.claim: LiPF6 decomposes at relatively low temperatures around 150–200 °C, releasing highly toxic and corrosive by-products such as hydrofluoric acid.
claims.quote: The most commonly used lithium salt, lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6), offers good ionic conductivity, but it decomposes at relatively low temperatures (around 150–200 °C), releasing highly toxic and corrosive by-products such as hydrofluoric acid

claims.claim: Moisture in the atmosphere can react with fluorinated decomposition products such as PF5 and POF3 to form hydrofluoric acid, increasing the corrosivity and toxicity of the emission plume during thermal abuse tests.
claims.quote: moisture in the atmosphere can react with fluorinated decomposition products (e.g., PF5 and POF3) to form hydrofluoric acid (HF), further increasing the corrosivity and toxicity of the emission plume

claims.claim: Prismatic cells consistently produce the largest volume of gaseous emissions during thermal runaway, whereas cylindrical cells generate the least.
claims.quote: prismatic cells consistently produce the largest volume of gaseous emissions, whereas cylindrical cells generate the least

claims.claim: At 25% SOC during thermal abuse tests in a semi-confined environment, elevated CO2 levels are observed despite the lower energy content, attributed to a slower reaction rate and prolonged combustion.
claims.quote: An exception occurs at 25% SOC, where elevated CO2 levels are observed despite the lower energy content; this is attributed to a slower reaction rate and prolonged combustion, which extends the duration of gas evolution while reducing the overall peak temperature and reaction intensity

claims.claim: Extinguishing an EV fire caused by LIBs can require up to 3 hours and 40 times more water compared to extinguishing a fire from an internal combustion engine that could be extinguished in only about 20 minutes.
claims.quote: Firefighters reported that extinguishing an EV fire caused by LIBs can require up to 3 h and 40 times more water compared to extinguishing a fire produced from an internal combustion engine that could be extinguished in only about 20 min

claims.claim: The venting temperature of LFP cylindrical cells (1500 mAh) is largely independent of the cell's SOC, while the peak temperature increases with higher SOC.
claims.quote: ARC test data presented by Bugryniec et al. indicate that the venting temperature of LFP cylindrical cells (1500 mAh) is largely independent of the cell's SOC, while the peak temperature increases with higher SOC

claims.claim: Aluminium melting point (660 °C) is much lower than copper melting point (1083 °C), causing aluminium to be mobilised and released from failing lithium-ion cells during thermal runaway while copper is less likely to be released in the same manner.
claims.quote: Copper, with its much higher melting point (1083 1C as compared to 660 1C for aluminium) is less likely to have been released from the cell in this manner.

claims.claim: In failure events involving multiple cells that generate higher temperatures, more significant levels of copper are expected to be seen in the smoke and residues compared to single-cell events.
claims.quote: It may be that in failure events involving multiple cells, where which generate higher temperatures, that more significant levels of copper are seen in the smoke and in the residues.

claims.claim: The proportion of aluminium observed in solid residues was greater than in smoke particulate for cells C, D, and H, attributed to larger-sized globules of molten aluminium that condense or rapidly react in air to form alumina compounds.
claims.quote: It is clear that in many of these samples particularly in cells C, D and H, the proportion of aluminium observed in the solid residue is greater than that in the smoke particulate. This is most likely due to the larger sized globules of molten aluminium ejected which will either condense or rapidly react in air to form alumina compounds.

claims.claim: For LCO cathode cells F and G, there was less aluminium in the residue than in the smoke particulate, attributed to higher temperatures generated by LCO cathode failure causing wider dispersal of aluminium.
claims.quote: for cells F and G there is less aluminium in the residue than the smoke; these cells have an LCO cathode-the higher temperatures generated by this cathode upon failure, and the more violent events observed may suggest that the aluminium is more likely to be widely dispersed when these cells fail.

claims.claim: The proportion of nickel in smoke particulate appeared slightly higher than in the solid residues across the full range of cells tested.
claims.quote: the proportion of nickel in the smoke particulate seems slightly higher than in the residues.

claims.claim: NMC nail penetration resulted in the highest PM2.5 emission rate of 551 g/h and total PN emission rate of 2.1E+17 particles/h among all five tests.
claims.quote: This module resulted in the highest emission rates of 551 g/h of PM2.5 and 2.1E þ 17 particles/h (or 1.9E07 part./cm3) of total PN.

claims.claim: LFP modules subjected to nail penetration did not exhibit thermal runaway propagation to other cells, resulting in relatively less particle and gaseous emissions compared to overcharge and NMC nail penetration tests.
claims.quote: LFP modules subjected to nail penetration yielded relatively less emissions as propagation was not observed.

claims.claim: HF concentration during LFP overcharge tests reached a peak of 177 ppm, remaining above the NIOSH IDLH level of 30 ppm for several minutes under well-ventilated conditions.
claims.quote: the HF peak of 177 ppm is of the greatest concern. National Institute for Occupational Safety & Health (NIOSH) defines the level of HF that is immediately dangerous to life and health (IDLH) as 30 ppm, and the data show the measured concentration remaining above this level for several minutes even under these well-ventilated conditions.

claims.claim: No HF was observed during NMC nail penetration thermal runaway, while the most hazardous compound detected was formaldehyde, which exceeded its 20 ppm IDLH level for less than 5 seconds.
claims.quote: there was no HF observed in the NMC test at all, likely because of the use of an anion salt not containing fluoride... The most hazardous compound observed during NMC nail penetration test was formaldehyde, which was measured above its 20 ppm IDLH level for less than 5 s.

claims.claim: NMC nail penetration produced a peak CO2 value more than 20 times that observed in LFP overcharge tests, indicating a much larger mass of electrolyte combusted per second.
claims.quote: NMC nail penetration test resulted in a peak CO2 value that was more than 20 times that observed in LFP overcharge tests, indicating a much larger mass of electrolyte combusted per second.

claims.claim: Vent gas volume increased linearly with SOC for both LiCoO2 pouch cells (3.7 V, 4.8 Ah) and cylindrical cells (3.7 V, 2.6 Ah).
claims.quote: The vent gas volume and combustion energy increased linearly with SOC for the pouch and cylindrical cells.

claims.claim: Pouch cells heated at 20°C/min released an average of 14.1% more vent gas than pouch cells heated at 5°C/min, across tested SOCs.
claims.quote: When comparing cells heated at 20°C/min to cells heated at 5°C/min, across the tested SOCs, the pouch cells released an average of 14.1% more vent gas and had an average of 34.4% greater combustion energy

claims.claim: Cylindrical cells heated at 20°C/min released an average of 7.2% more vent gas and had an average of 13.0% greater combustion energy than cylindrical cells heated at 5°C/min, across tested SOCs.
claims.quote: the cylindrical released an average of 7.2% more vent gas and had an average of 13.0% greater combustion energy

claims.claim: Slower heating rates allow more time for electrolyte to boil and vent before thermal runaway, leaving less electrolyte at the time of thermal runaway, thereby reducing vent gas volume and combustion energy compared to faster heating rates.
claims.quote: This is because slower heating rates allow more time for the electrolyte inside of the cells to boil and vent than with cells heated at faster heating rates. Therefore, cells heated at slower rates have less electrolyte remaining in the cells once thermal runaway actually occurs which reduces the amount of vent gas and combustion energy over the same cells tested at faster heating rates.

claims.claim: At 30% SOC, the percent change in combustion energy between heating rates of 5°C/min and 20°C/min was 129% for pouch cells and 64% for cylindrical cells, representing the maximum percent change across all tested SOCs.
claims.quote: The maximum percent change in combustion energy between a pouch cell heated at 5°C/min and 20°C/min was 129% and occurred in cells charged to 30% SOC... At 30% SOC, the percent change in combustion energy between heating rates of 5°C/min and 20°C/min was 129% and 64% for the pouch and cylindrical cells

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the total difference in combustion energy between heating rates of 5°C/min and 20°C/min was 21.5 kJ for pouch cells and 5.9 kJ for cylindrical cells, representing the maximum total difference across all tested SOCs.
claims.quote: The maximum total difference in combustion energy between a pouch cell heated at 5°C/min and 20°C/min was 21.5 kJ and occurred in cells charged to 100% SOC... At 100% SOC, the total difference between heating rates of 5°C/min and 20°C/min in combustion energy was 21.5 kJ and 5.9 kJ for the pouch and cylindrical cells

claims.claim: The top three vent gas constituents by volume for both pouch and cylindrical LiCoO2 cells across all SOCs were carbon dioxide, hydrogen, and carbon monoxide, contributing 81±2%vol and 54±12%vol of the total gas mixture for pouch and cylindrical cells, respectively.
claims.quote: The top three constituents as a percentage of the vent gas by volume across all SOCs for the pouch and cylindrical cells were carbon dioxide, hydrogen, and carbon monoxide. On average, the three gases contribute to 81±2%vol and 54±12%vol of the total gas mixture for pouch cells and cylindrical cells, respectively.

claims.claim: The percentage of carbon dioxide by volume in vent gas decreased while carbon monoxide by volume increased with increasing SOC for both pouch and cylindrical cells.
claims.quote: Measurements show that the percentage of carbon dioxide by volume decreased while carbon monoxide by volume increased with increasing SOCs

claims.claim: The average concentration of hydrogen by volume in vent gas across all SOCs was 24±4%vol for pouch cells and 12±4%vol for cylindrical cells.
claims.quote: The average concentration of hydrogen by volume in vent gas across all SOCs for the pouch and cylindrical cells were 24±4%vol and 12±4%vol, respectively.

claims.claim: Testing suggests that 8.59%vol Halon 1301 is required to render all ratios of premixed lithium-ion battery vent gas in air inert, whereas the 5%vol knockdown and 3%vol sustained concentrations used in Class C cargo compartments may be insufficient.
claims.quote: Testing suggests that 8.59 %vol Halon 1301 is required to render all ratios of the Li-Ion pBVG in air inert

claims.claim: A previous FAA study statistically determined that heating rates at or above 15°C/min were more likely to produce a violent thermal runaway reaction than cells heated at less than 15°C/min, with critical thresholds defined as a maximum cell case temperature above 250°C and the release of over 0.5 L of vent gas.
claims.quote: heating rates at or above 15°C/min were more likely to produce a violent thermal runaway reaction than cells heated at less than 15°C/min. The critical thresholds for a violent thermal runaway reaction were determined to be a maximum cell case temperature above 250°C and the release of over 0.5 L of vent gas

claims.claim: Combustion energy for pouch cells ranged from 5.2 kJ at 30% SOC and 5°C/min to 114 kJ at 100% SOC and 20°C/min; for cylindrical cells it ranged from 4.5 kJ at 30% SOC and 5°C/min to 42 kJ at 100% SOC and 20°C/min.
claims.quote: The combustion energy for the pouch and cylindrical cells ranged from 5.2 kJ and 4.5 kJ, respectively, when charged to 30% SOC and heated at 5°C/min, to 114 kJ and 42 kJ, respectively, when charged to 100% SOC and heated at 20°C/min.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway is an uncontrolled and self-propagating exothermic reaction within a lithium-ion battery that leads to rapid increase in temperature, venting of combustible gases, and potential thermal events.
claims.quote: Thermal runaway is an uncontrolled and self-propagating exothermic reaction within the battery that leads to rapid increase in temperature, venting of combustible gases and potential thermal events

claims.claim: The NCA gas mixture is composed of 10% CO2, 37.1% CO, 42.8% H2, 7.1% CH4, and 3% C2H4, while the LFP gas mixture is composed of 48.4% CO2, 29.5% CO, 5.6% H2, 7% CH4, and 0.5% C2H6.
claims.quote: Gas Mixtures CO2 CO H2 CH4 C2H4 C2H6 NCA 10 37.1 42.8 7.1 3 0 LFP 48.4 29.5 5.6 7 0 0.5

claims.claim: At room temperature, the breakdown voltage of the NCA gas mixture is higher than that of the LFP gas mixture for the majority of electrode separation distances, but at 300 °C the opposite is true.
claims.quote: it can be seen that the breakdown voltage of NCA is higher than that of LFP for the majority of the separation distances at room temperature. Interestingly, the opposite is true for the two gas mixtures at 300 °C.

claims.claim: For the LFP gas mixture, the breakdown voltage at room temperature (19 °C) is notably lower than at 200 °C and 300 °C up to approximately 1.5 mm electrode separation distance, after which the values converge.
claims.quote: For the LFP gas mixture, the breakdown voltage at room temperature (19 °C) is notably lower than it is at 200 and 300 °C until approximately 1.5mm separation distance. Around this point, the values converge and follow a similar trend.

claims.claim: The sustainability of flame combustion from ignition of the NCA gas mixture under fuel-rich conditions is driven by H radicals rather than OH radicals, indicating the combustion process can be sustained even where there is a low amount of oxygen present.
claims.quote: This presents a crucial conclusion: The sustainability of a flame resulting from ignition of the NCA gas mixture is not driven by OH, but rather by H. This indicates the combustion process can occur and be sustained even where there is a low amount of oxygen present.

claims.claim: The TR trigger temperature of LFP battery is approximately 184°C, which is higher than that of NCM ternary batteries, which range from approximately 130–143°C.
claims.quote: The TR trigger temperature of LFP battery is about 180 °C, which is higher than that of the NCM ternary battery, which is around 130–140 °C.

claims.claim: Under standard conditions, the normalized gas production of NCM ternary batteries is 1.8–2.8 L/Ah, while that of LFP batteries is significantly lower at only 0.569 L/Ah.
claims.quote: The gas production of the NCM battery is between 1.814–2.752 L/Ah after normalization, while the gas production of the LFP battery is significantly lower at 0.569 L/Ah.

claims.claim: Based on gas production volume, the degree of harm caused by thermal runaway is ranked as: NCM9 0.5 0.5 > NCM811 > NCM622 > NCM523 > LFP.
claims.quote: Based on battery gas production, the degree of harm caused by thermal runaway can be ranked as follows: NCM9 0.5 0.5 > NCM811 > NCM622 > NCM523 > LFP.

claims.claim: In the gas production of LFP batteries during thermal runaway, H2 accounts for 50.82% while CO only accounts for 9.3%, resulting in a lower lower flammability limit (LFL) compared to NCM battery TR gas.
claims.quote: In the gas production of LFP batteries, H2 accounts for 50.82%, while CO only accounts for 9.3%... The TR formation gas of the LFP battery contains a higher amount of H2 compared to the NCM battery, resulting in a lower LFL due to the relatively low LFL of H2.

claims.claim: From the perspective of thermal runaway gas composition and flammability, the hazard ranking is LFP > NCM811 > NCM622 > NCM523 > NCM9 0.5 0.5, which is the reverse of the ranking based on gas production volume.
claims.quote: Therefore, from the perspective of thermal runaway gas composition, the hazard ranking is LFP > NCM811 > NCM622 > NCM523 > NCM9 0.5 0.5.

claims.claim: When the excess air coefficient is around 0.9, the flame propagation changes from slow deflagration to fast deflagration, which causes a high risk of explosion for the battery pack.
claims.quote: When the excess air coefﬁcient is around 0.9, the ﬂame propagation changes from a slow deﬂagration to a fast deﬂagration, which causes a high risk of explosion for the battery pack.

claims.claim: When the initial temperature of the venting gas mixture is 400 K, the flame velocity exceeds the local sonic speed and the combustion mode changes from slow deflagration to fast deflagration, posing a high risk of fast deflagration and even explosion of the battery pack.
claims.quote: Therefore, if the venting gas of a lithium-ion battery mixes with the air and the temperature is higher than 400 K, there is a high risk of fast deﬂagration and even the explosion of the battery pack.

claims.claim: As initial pressure increases from 81 kPa to 174 kPa, the maximum average flame velocity increases from 93.5 m/s to 151.5 m/s, with no change in combustion mode.
claims.quote: The maximum average ﬂame velocities are 93.5 m·s−1, 100 m·s−1, 138.2 m·s−1, and 151.5 m·s−1, corresponding to an initial pressure of 81 kPa, 101 kPa, 154 kPa, and 174 kPa. A similar trend is displayed with the propagation time. However, no deﬂagration appears, and the combustion mode is not changed.

claims.claim: As initial temperature increases from 300 K to 400 K, the maximum average flame velocity increases from 109.2 m/s to 402.5 m/s, with the influence of initial temperature being more acute than that of initial pressure.
claims.quote: At the initial temperature of 300 K, 329 K, 360 K, and 400 K, the maximum average ﬂame velocity is 109.2 m·s−1, 138.2 m·s−1, 264.7 m·s−1, and 402.5 m·s−1, respectively. The results imply a high sensitivity of the initial temperature to the ﬂame velocity of the venting gas.

claims.claim: When the excess air coefficient decreases from 1.2 to 0.8, the maximum average flame velocity increases from 95.25 m/s to 445 m/s, with the maximum velocity achieved at excess air coefficient of 0.8 in a slightly fuel-rich mixture.
claims.quote: When the excess air coefﬁcient decreases from 1.2 to 0.7, the maximum average ﬂame velocity increases from 95.25 m·s−1 to 437.5 m·s−1. The average ﬂame velocity is larger in a slightly fuel-rich mixture. The maximum velocity arrives at 445 m·s−1 when the excess air coefﬁcient is 0.8.

claims.claim: For a 50 Ah LFP battery at 100% SOC, when the battery surface temperature reaches between 190–210 °C, the temperature increase rate accelerates to 37 °C/min and the voltage decreases to zero instantly.
claims.quote: When the surface temperature of the battery reaches between 190–210 °C, the thermal regulation of the battery is activated, the temperature increase rate accelerates to 37 °C/min, and the voltage decreases to zero instantly.

claims.claim: The maximum battery surface temperature during thermal runaway of a 50 Ah LFP cell reached approximately 320 °C at 100% SOC under external heating.
claims.quote: Eventually, the battery temperature reaches a maximum of around 320 °C.

claims.claim: At 0% SOC, the LFP battery safety valve opened but no major secondary eruption occurred, whereas a second eruption occurred at both 50% and 100% SOC.
claims.quote: At 0% SOC, the battery valve was opened and there was no major secondary eruption. A second eruption occurred at 50% and 100% SOC, and the manifested traits are largely constant.

claims.claim: The severity of thermal runaway in LFP batteries increased with increasing SOC, with maximum temperatures following the order 100% TMAX > 50% TMAX > 0% TMAX.
claims.quote: The severity of the thermal runaway increased with increasing SOC, as shown by the graphic in Figure 4, 100% TMAX > 50% TMAX > 0% TMAX.

claims.claim: H2 accounts for approximately 50% of the normalized gas volume generated by 50 Ah LFP batteries during thermal runaway, making it the dominant gas component by proportion.
claims.quote: From the perspective of gas production, H2 accounts for a relatively high proportion of the gas generated by lithium iron phosphate batteries, approaching about 50%.

claims.claim: The mass loss rate of 50 Ah LFP batteries during thermal runaway increased with SOC: 16% at 0% SOC, 17% at 50% SOC, and 18% at 100% SOC.
For LFP cells, the first valve opening occurs at approximately 160 °C, before the onset of thermal runaway, as identified by the intersection point of temperature rise rate tangents.
claims.quote: A tangent with multiple stages of temperature rise rate can be found in the vicinity of 160 °C at the junctures... the lithium iron phosphate battery valve opens before thermal runaway.

claims.claim: LFP cells exhibited a maximum cell temperature of 305 °C during thermal runaway, compared to NCA cells which can reach maximum temperatures of 1075 °C.
claims.quote: NCA cells can reach maximum temperatures of 1075 °C. In this study, LFP cells exhibited a less pronounced thermal runaway with maximum cell temperatures as high as 305 °C.

claims.claim: During the second eruption zone of a 50 Ah LFP battery, battery temperature ranges from 200 to 300 °C, a considerable quantity of gas is expelled, and both temperature and gas quantity reach their maximum.
claims.quote: Second eruption zone: The temperature of the battery ranges from 200 to 300 °C. At this point, a considerable quantity of gas is expelled from the battery as a result of a strong reaction. At this point, the battery temperature reaches its maximum, and the quantity of gas also reaches its maximum and becomes steady.

claims.claim: The interior pressure of the sealed experimental chamber underwent its first abrupt increase at a rate of 110 kPa/s when the safety valve opened during the first eruption stage of the 100% SOC LFP cell.
claims.quote: the battery's surface temperature remains mostly unchanged, but the experimental chamber's interior pressure undergoes its first abrupt increase, rising to 110 kPa/s.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the normalized gas amount for NCM batteries ranges within 0.075–0.105 mol/Ah, while for LFP batteries it is only 0.025–0.035 mol/Ah, implying NCM batteries generate more than around 1–3 times the gases in moles compared to LFP batteries.
claims.quote: In regard to NCM batteries, the normalized gas amount varies within 0.075–0.105 mol/Ah (1.68–2.35 L/Ah), consistent with 0.09 mol/Ah [18] and 0.08 mol/Ah [17] to a certain degree, while that for LFP batteries is only 0.025–0.035 mol/L (0.56–0.78 L/Ah), close to 0.03 mol/Ah, found in previous work [17], implying that NCM batteries generate more than around 1–3 times the gases in moles compared to LFP batteries.

claims.claim: The maximum gas releasing rate shows a strongly positive correlation with battery capacity for NCM batteries (ranging 0.04–0.31 mol/s) and only a slight increase for LFP batteries (0.02–0.06 mol/s) at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: the maximum gas releasing rate presents a strongly positive correlation with the capacity of NCM batteries (0.04–0.31 mol/s) and a slight increase for LFP batteries (0.02–0.06 mol/s)

claims.claim: For 117 Ah prismatic NCM811 batteries, a venting event occurring at elevated temperatures (160–250 °C) approximately doubles the maximum gas releasing rate compared to room temperature, both at 50% SOC (0.374 mol/s vs. 0.185 mol/s) and at 100% SOC (0.481 mol/s vs. 0.219 mol/s).
claims.quote: A venting event occurring at elevated temperatures tends to erupt more violently and increase by double, compared to either 0.374 mol/s under 160–250 degrees Celsius with 0.185 mol/s at room temperature for 50% SOC or 0.481 mol/s under 160–250 degrees Celsius with 0.219 mol/s at room temperature for 100% SOC.

claims.claim: For NCM811 prismatic batteries at elevated ambient temperature, gas production grew around 10% and characteristic venting durations reduced to approximately half compared to results at normal temperatures, for both 50% SOC and 100% SOC cases.
claims.quote: The gas production of both the 50% SOC case and the 100% SOC case grew around 10% at elevated temperatures, and the characteristic durations reduced to half the results at normal temperatures.

claims.claim: A positive correlation of total gas amount and maximum gas releasing rate with SOC was observed for both LFP and NCM811 batteries, and for LFP batteries all venting duration parameters decreased monotonously with increasing SOC.
claims.quote: A positive correlation of total gas amount and maximum gas releasing rate with SOC was satisﬁed for both LFP and NCM811 batteries... With regard to the four parameters for venting durations, LFP batteries showed a prominent trend where all parameters decreased monotonously with SOC.

claims.claim: For NCA cells subjected to thermal abuse at 100% SoC and 5°C/min, CO was the most abundant species released at maximum temperature, reaching 2.25 × 10^5 ppmv.
claims.quote: For NCA cells, CO is the most abundant species released (2.25 × 105 ppmv).

claims.claim: For LFP cells subjected to thermal abuse at 100% SoC and 5°C/min, CO was entirely converted to CO2, with CO concentration being negligible (nil ppm) and CO2 concentration at 1.65 × 10^5 ppmv.
claims.quote: For LFP cells, CO is entirely converted to CO2, with the CO concentration being negligible (nil ppm) and a CO2 concentration at 1.65 × 105 ppmv.

claims.claim: The complete conversion of CO to CO2 in LFP cells is attributed to the higher stability of the LFP cathode material, which remains stable up to 500°C, a temperature not reached even during thermal runaway (maximum cell surface temperature around 310°C).
claims.quote: This behavior is attributed to the higher stability of the LFP cathode material, which remains stable up to 500 °C [24], a temperature not reached even during TR (maximum cell surface temperature around 310 °C).

claims.claim: Regardless of cell chemistry (NCA, LTO, or LFP) at 100% SoC, the mean HF concentration over 30 minutes exceeded the IDLH limit of 30 ppm, with values of 49 ± 1 ppm for NCA, 101 ± 12 ppm for LTO, and 110 ± 23 ppm for LFP.
claims.quote: The data in Table 5 indicate that, regardless of the chemical composition, the concentration values exceed the IDLH values of 30 ppm for HF [31] and 1200 ppm for CO [30] over a 30 min period as set by NIOSH.

claims.claim: NCA cells reached a maximum temperature of 511 ± 96°C and pressure of 5.071 barg during thermal runaway, which is over 200°C higher and approximately 3 barg greater than LTO (381 ± 89°C, 1.802 barg) and LFP (406 ± 62°C, 2.202 barg) cells under identical thermal abuse conditions.
claims.quote: The maximum temperatures and pressures reached at the peak were 511 ± 96 ◦C and 5.071 barg for NCA, 381 ± 89 ◦C and 1.802 barg for LTO and 406 ± 62 ◦C and 2.202 barg for LFP. The differences in maximum temperature and pressure are significant, with NCA cells reaching over 200 ◦C higher temperatures compared to LTO and LFP cells.
